ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hong Kong-style stir-fried noodles; FOODON_00002802 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Noodles, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for Hong Kong-style stir-fried noodles with mixed toppings.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for Hong Kong-style stir-fried noodles.

main_ingredients: thin egg noodles or canton noodles

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Hong Kong Noodles is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a single plate of stir-fried noodles with mixed vegetables and protein.

nutrient_source.title: Pancit Canton / Stir-fried egg noodles with mixed vegetables and meat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table analogous estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Filipino-style Hong Kong Noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 168918), stir-fried mixed vegetables, and lean pork/chicken, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA composition data for pancit-type dishes. The dish is nutritionally analogous to Filipino pancit canton: thin egg noodles stir-fried with soy sauce, oyster sauce, mixed vegetables, and mixed protein. Per-100g values reflect a typical cooked composite.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — egg noodles, cooked (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Filipino-style Hong Kong Noodles. GI of 47 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked egg noodles (GI ~47, low category), which is the primary noodle component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (47 × 55.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 26.1, rounded to 26.0. The stir-frying process and mixed vegetable/protein content are consistent with a low-to-medium GI outcome; GI 47 is a conservative defensible estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1440 mg per standard 300 g serving) from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sodium soy sauce or oyster sauce preparations, as a single serving can contribute approximately 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (~55.5 g per 300 g serving) with low GI (~47) from egg noodles, partially offset by protein and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include Hong Kong Noodles in a meal plan with careful portion control, as the low GI of egg noodles produces a more gradual postprandial glucose rise compared to rice-based dishes, but the total glycemic load per serving remains moderate at approximately 26.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat content; cooking oil contributes to total fat intake
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Hong Kong Noodles should be consumed in reduced portions with attention to sodium from condiments and total fat from cooking oil, and lean protein sources such as chicken breast or shrimp are preferable to fatty pork cuts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hong Kong Noodles, known in Filipino cuisine as a Cantonese-influenced stir-fried noodle dish, consists of thin egg noodles (canton noodles) stir-fried in a hot wok with soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, mixed vegetables such as cabbage and carrots, and a protein component such as chicken, pork, or shrimp. The dish is a staple of Filipino Chinese-influenced cooking and is closely related to Pancit Canton, differing primarily in the use of a slightly thinner noodle and a more pronounced soy-oyster sauce glaze. The texture is tender yet slightly chewy, with a savory umami flavor profile derived from the combination of soy and oyster sauces. In Singapore, it is served at Filipino-operated cafes and casual dining establishments. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 426 kcal, 21.6 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 55.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is clinically significant at approximately 1440 mg per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index of approximately 47 (egg noodle base) makes it a more favorable carbohydrate choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes compared to white rice dishes, though total glycemic load per serving remains moderate. The dish contains gluten, egg, soy, and shellfish allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard preparation.
common_user_queries: Is Hong Kong Noodles the same as Pancit Canton?
How many calories in Hong Kong Noodles?
Is Hong Kong Noodles good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Filipino-style Hong Kong Noodles exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked egg noodle data and FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous pancit-type dish data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked egg noodles. Confidence is medium because the dish composition is well-understood and the analogous sources are defensible, but no direct measurement of this specific dish exists in a primary approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked egg noodles, FDC ID 168918) and FAO/INFOODS SEA pancit-type dish analogues; no exact primary source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries used. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries used. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous source entries used. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for this food type; no hydrogenated fats in standard preparation). 6. GI of 47 is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 7. The cuisine tag 'Filipino' from the input is preserved; the dish is a Filipino adaptation of Cantonese-style stir-fried noodles. 8. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce and potential pork content make standard preparation non-halal; halal-certified variants may exist. 9. The input food_name 'HongKong Noodle' has been normalized to the canonical English form 'Hong Kong Noodles'. 10. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean cuisine mandatory languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore.
